In addition to using calibrated questions in order to spot liars, deal with jerks, and charm everyone else, I want to cover some other tactics in this chapter. These rules, methods, tactics, and tools will use both verbal and nonverbal forms of communication to modify the mental state of your counterpart. With these tools, you'll be able to achieve your next level of negotiating. The first concept is the 73855 rule, which represents the components of how a message is delivered. Basically, the concept is roughly 7% of a message is carried by the words, while 38% comes from the tone of voice, and 55% from the speaker's body language and facial expressions. The most effective way to use this rule is to pay close attention to the alignment of these cues and then changes in that alignment. When tone and body language don't align with the literal meaning of the words, you've probably got a problem. As you pay attention to these changes in the alignment, probe those moments a little more. The gamut of reasons can range from outright lying to discomfort with what's going on in the moment. Be careful of jumping to a conclusion too soon, as it could easily be the wrong one. Treat your findings like a hypothesis. It requires testing before conclusion. So when you notice that someone's tone of voice or body language doesn't match the meaning of their words, you want to use labels to uncover the reason. Your counterpart will be appreciative because you are respectfully acknowledging their reaction without cornering them. Next, we have the rule of three. All you're doing here is getting your counterpart to agree to the same thing three times in the same portion of the conversation. Occasionally, you'll find yourself having stumbled into sealing a deal with a yes. It's pretty much impossible to repeat counterfeit yeses three times without a significant flinch, or break in the alignment of the 738-55 rule we just talked about. Lies are so used to getting you hook-lined and sinkered with just one counterfeit yes, they'll be shocked if they have to repeat it. That shock will show up in a physical tell or flinch, a change in the alignment of the 738-55 rule. Now, you might be thinking that using the rule of three, you're going to come across like a broken record. The key to avoiding this is to employ a variety of tactics. An example of how this might go is, number one, this would be the first time they agree to something or give you a commitment. This may come as a result of a label. So it sounds like you're good with this. Number two, paraphrase the agreement. Watch for the flinch here. If you see it, simply label this slowly, gently, and downward inflecting. It seems like there's a problem. If no flinch, number three, summarize what they said in order to get a that's right. So you've got no problem with, follow with a calibrated how or what question about implementation that asks them to lay out what success looks like. I will tell you that when I really want to be sure of something, we avoid yes like the plague, because closing with a no is so much more firm. Do you disagree? Are you against making a firm commitment now? Are our go-to moves? The third concept is something called the Pinocchio effect. I'm sure you're familiar with the wooden puppet who wanted to be a real boy. Every time he lied, his nose would grow, which made it pretty obvious he wasn't telling the truth. What we're looking for here is more words. The harder someone works to convince you because they know they're lying, the more words they use. It's as simple as that. They may even add complexity to their sentences. Actor and comedian W.C. Fields nailed this concept when he talked about baffling people with BS. Next time someone tries to persuade you with more words, convoluted sentences, and complex words, you should be on your guard. Another concept is your counterpart's usage of pronouns. It's important for reasons other than revealing liars. It can show how important someone is in the deal-making process. The more your counterpart uses I, me, and my singular pronouns, the less important they are. The opposite is also true. If your counterpart avoids using personal pronouns, like we and us, they are an important person. The reason for this is decision-makers want to avoid being cornered on the spot, so they defer to people away from the table to keep from getting pinned down. Excessive usage of first-person pronouns means less important, while second or third-person pronouns reveal larger influence in the decision-making chain. The fifth and final concept to go over in this chapter is something called the name discount. While people like you to remember and use their name, they don't like to be hammered with it. A tendency of the slick salesperson is to bludgeon you with your name over and over again. The name discount is nothing more than using your own name instead. It introduces the dynamic of forced empathy because it immediately causes your counterpart to see you as a unique person, someone with a name, as opposed to a piece of matter in the universe. Sequencing is important here. Don't go for the name discount right up front. Do you have a, insert your name, discount? Should not be the first thing out of your mouth. Preced it with a great manner. If you've made them laugh or they reveal something personal, this is an indicator you can use the name discount. Be creative and have fun with it. Smile. Negotiations don't have to be only business. You're allowed to enjoy yourself and the conversation. Let them enjoy the conversation too. At the worst, you've brightened somebody else's day, and you might just get your own special discount.
